title: Mumbai Reservoirs at 9.3% — Lowest in Three Years — as BMC Bans Potable Water for Construction and Pools
summary: Mumbai's seven lakes hold just 1.55 lakh million litres against a total capacity of 14.5 lakh million litres — a reservoir level the BMC has called 'critical,' the lowest for this date in three years. The civic body has banned potable water use for vehicle washing, construction, swimming pools, and gardening, and directed the Railways and Navy to shift to treated sewage water. A 10% residential cut and 20% commercial cut are in effect, with construction sites and pools facing a full supply shutoff. The monsoon's arrival has now been pushed to around June 23–25.

coverages.headline: 40 days to empty: Inside Mumbai's desperate race to conserve water as reservoirs fall below 10%
coverages.excerpt: Mumbai's seven lakes hold just 1.55 lakh million litres against a capacity of 14.5 lakh million litres; BMC has banned use of potable water for vehicles, construction, pools and gardening, and directed Railways and Navy to shift to treated sewage water.
coverages.bias_notes: The exact reservoir figures (1.55 lakh million litres against 14.5 lakh million litre capacity) give this story real precision — a 9.34% reservoir level, calculated against three years of historical data, makes 'lowest in three years for this date' a verifiable claim rather than a vague alarm. The directive for Railways and Navy to shift to treated sewage water is the most concrete and least-reported detail here; it signals that even institutional users with their own infrastructure are being forced into supply substitution, which says more about the severity of the crisis than residential cuts alone. The piece is appropriately urgent without resorting to the kind of speculative '45 days of water left' framing used in earlier June coverage of the same underlying reservoir trend — '40 days to empty' here is at least anchored to the headline's own specific math.
